user_query:
I'm compiling a small, source-backed dataset on the cultural significance of several East Asian landmarks for my thesis. Please implement a runnable script that uses live Internet search to discover and retrieve official descriptive pages for each landmark in input/landmarks.csv, extracts structured fields from the pages, and produces both data files and a brief status report I can share with my advisor.

Requirements:
- Read the list of landmarks from input/landmarks.csv.
- For each landmark, perform search engine queries that combine the landmark name (and any aliases provided) with a site filter for at least one official source appropriate to the country. Accepted official domains/patterns include:
  - UNESCO World Heritage Centre (whc.unesco.org)
  - Japan’s Agency for Cultural Affairs (domains under bunka.go.jp, including subdomains)
  - Korea’s Cultural Heritage Administration (heritage.go.kr)
  - China’s government cultural heritage domains (e.g., ncha.gov.cn or other .gov.cn pages clearly tied to cultural heritage)
- Use search engine queries (not hardcoded URLs) to find and follow candidate result links. Record every query you try and the result URLs you considered.
- Download up to the first 3 relevant official pages you find per landmark (across all official domains), saving each page’s raw HTML.
- From each saved page, extract structured data: url, domain, page_title (<title>), meta_description (if present), first_paragraph (first non-empty paragraph text, trimmed to 300 characters), landmark (as in input), country (as in input), source_type classified by domain (UNESCO | JP_National | KR_National | CN_Gov | OtherOfficial), and fetch_timestamp (ISO-8601).
- Aggregate per landmark with counts by source_type and include a sample_snippet chosen by priority UNESCO > National (JP/KR/CN) > OtherOfficial.
- Compose a short status report summarizing coverage and next steps.

Deliverables (create all of these under the workspace):
- scripts/fetch_official_pages.py (or equivalent executable script) that performs the workflow above.
- logs/search_queries.jsonl — one JSON object per attempted query-result pair with fields: landmark, country, query, url, rank (integer if available), timestamp.
- data/raw_html/ — raw HTML files for fetched pages (name files to include the landmark slug, domain, and a timestamp).
- data/pages.jsonl — one JSON object per saved page with the extracted fields listed above.
- data/landmarks_summary.csv — one row per landmark with columns: landmark, country, pages_total, pages_unesco, pages_jp_national, pages_kr_national, pages_cn_gov, pages_other_official, has_official (boolean), sample_snippet.
- reports/status_report.md — a brief, human-readable update that includes: (1) 1–2 paragraphs summarizing what was gathered and any notable gaps, (2) a concise coverage list showing for each landmark whether an official page was captured from UNESCO or a national agency, and (3) a short “Next steps” section with 2–3 concrete actions.

Constraints:
- Use live Internet search; do not hardcode page URLs. Ensure queries include site filters matching the official domain patterns above.
- Ensure every output file listed is created by running your script, and that records in data/pages.jsonl and data/landmarks_summary.csv can be traced back to both the input CSV and the saved raw HTML.
- Keep the implementation self-contained and runnable in a normal local environment without special credentials.

Input path: input/landmarks.csv
Output paths to create: scripts/fetch_official_pages.py, logs/search_queries.jsonl, data/raw_html/, data/pages.jsonl, data/landmarks_summary.csv, reports/status_report.md

Thank you—this will help me compare how different official bodies frame each site’s cultural significance while giving my advisor a clear progress update. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
